---
title: Lab 3
---

# Overview

## Today

* [What is flask?](#flask)
* [Web development resources](#resources)
* [Create your first flask app](#first)
    * [Install flask](#install)
    * [Set-up your flask app](#setup)
    * [Run your flask app](#run)
    * [Make changes to your app](#changes)
    * [Create new pages](#newpages)
    * [Pass variables to your webpage](#variables)
    * [Use templates](#templates)
    * [Some HTML and CSS basics](#html)
* [Exercises](#exercises)

## Learning Objectives

By completing this lab, you will be able to:

1. Set up a Flask development environment with virtual environments
2. Create routes that handle different URL patterns
3. Pass data between Python backend and HTML templates
4. Structure a multi-page application with template inheritance
5. *(Foundation for future labs)* Understand how to expose Python functions via HTTP endpoints

Prerequisites: This lab uses Python classes and objects (e.g., `Flask(__name__)`). If you need a refresher on OOP, see the bootcamp materials.

## Why Web Development in a Data Structures & Algorithms Course?

You might wonder why we're learning the **web framework Flask** in a data structures and algorithms course. Reasons include:

- Practical Application: Many algorithms you'll learn this semester (e.g. sorting, searching, graph traversal) need interfaces. Building a simple web app lets you visualize and interact with your algorithms.

- API Design: Later labs will have you build **REST APIs** that expose algorithmic functionality. Understanding Flask routing is essential for this.

- Data Flow: Web apps provide an opportunity to demonstrate key CS concepts, such as request/response cycles, state management, and the client-server model.

- Final Project Foundation: Your course project will involve deploying an algorithm as a web service. This lab (and Flask in general) provides a foundation.

## Additional Resources <a class="anchor" id="resources"></a>

- [Flask Quickstart](https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/3.1.x/quickstart/)
- [Jinja2 Templates](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/templates/)
- [HTML Cheatsheet](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Guides/Cheatsheet)
- [CSS Quickstart](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Getting_started/Your_first_website/Styling_the_content)
- [CSS Overview and Tutorials](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Core/Styling_basics)

# What is flask and why should we use it? <a class="anchor" id="flask"></a>

## What is Flask?

Flask is a **microframework** for building web applications in Python. It was created by Armin Ronacher in 2010 and has grown into one of the most widely used Python web frameworks (alongside Django).

Under the hood, Flask is built on two libraries that handle the hard parts for you:

- **Werkzeug**: the **WSGI** toolkit that receives raw HTTP requests from the network and turns them into Python objects your code can work with.
- **Jinja2**: the templating engine that lets you build HTML pages with dynamic Python data embedded in them (more on this in section 7).

"Micro" means minimal by design, not underpowered or limited. Flask ships with just the essentials: routing, request handling, and templating. Unlike Django, it does not include a database layer, admin interface, or built-in authentication.

Flask is also **"Pythonic"**. Its design typically follows Python's philosophy of readability, simplicity, and explicitness. You will recognise Python idioms, such as decorators, keyword arguments, and context managers, throughout Flask's API. If you understand Python, Flask will feel natural.

### How Flask Works: The Request-Response Cycle

Like most web frameworks, Flask is is built around one fundamental idea: a browser sends a **request** and the server sends back a **response**.

Flask's job is to:

1. Listen for incoming **HTTP requests** on a **port** (default: 5000)
2. Match the URL to the right Python function (**routing**)
3. Call that function and collect its return value
4. Wrap the return value in an **HTTP response** and send it back to the requestor

An example of a request-response is:

1. The browser/client sends `GET /hello` to the Flask server.
2. The Flask server calls the `hello()` function.
3. The `hello()` function returns `"<h1>Hello!</h1>"`.
4. Flask bundles this into a HTTP response and sends it to the requesting browser/client.
5. The browser renders the HTML.

### Key Concepts

#### Route

A **route** maps a URL pattern to a Python function. Routes are defined using the `@app.route("/path")` **decorator**. When a Flask application receives an HTTP request, it scans its **routing table** for a pattern matching the requested URL and calls the associated function. For example, `@app.route("/hello")` means "whenever someone visits `/hello`, call this function." Routes can include variable segments like `<city>` to capture parts of the URL as arguments. You've probably seen this before with `?` suffixes in URLs.

#### View function

A **view function** is the Python function connected to a route. It runs when its routemis matched, and its return value becomes the HTTP response body sent back to the browser. The function's job is to decide what the user sees. View functions can return plain text, HTML strings, rendered templates, or JSON. The term "view" comes from the **model-view-controller (MVC) architectural pattern** (described below).

#### Template

A **template** is an HTML file with special syntax that Flask processes and fills in with data before sending it to the browser. Instead of building HTML strings inside Python, you write a normal `.html` file and mark the spots where **dynamic content** goes. This keeps your Python logic and your HTML presentation cleanly separated.

#### Jinja2

**Jinja2** is the templating engine Flask uses to process template files. It extends plain HTML with three tag types: `{{ expr }}` to output a value, `{% statement %}` for control flow (e.g. loops and conditionals), and `{# comment #}` for comments. The syntax is deliberately similar to Python, so there is very little new syntax to learn. Jinja2 is also used by many other tools.

#### WSGI

**Web Server Gateway Interface (WSGI)** is a standard protocol that defines how a web server passes HTTP requests to an application and how the application sends responses back. Flask implements this **interface** via Werkzeug. In development you use Flask's built-in WSGI server, while in production you would use a real WSGI server in front of your Flask app. You rarely interact with WSGI directly. Think of it as the plumbing that makes everything connect.

#### Debug mode

**Debug mode** is a development-only setting that automatically reloads the server whenever you save a `.py` file (so you don't need to restart manually) and displays a detailed interactive traceback in the browser when your code raises an **exception**. Don't enable debug mode in production, as the interactive console allows for arbitrary Python code execution in the browser, which is useful for debugging and experimenting but beyond terrible for security.

### Flask and the MVC Pattern

Most web frameworks are loosely organised around the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern. MVC divides a program into three layers of responsibility so that (ideally) changes in one do not (directly) affect the others.

| Layer | Responsibility | In Flask |
|---|---|---|
| **Model** | Data and business logic | Your Python code: algorithms, data classes, database queries |
| **View** | What the user sees | Jinja2 templates (`.html` files in `templates/`) |
| **Controller** | Receives requests, coordinates Model and View | Flask routes and view functions |

Flask does not enforce/require MVC but the pattern maps naturally onto every Flask app:

1. A browser sends a request to a URL.
2. Flask's router (the **Controller**) matches the URL and calls the right view function.
3. The view function runs whatever Python logic it needs (the **Model**), such as sorting a list, querying a database, or computing a Fibonacci number.
4. It passes the result to a Jinja2 template (the **View**), which renders it as HTML.
5. Flask sends the HTML back to the browser.

Keeping these three layers separate pays off as your apps grow in size and complexity. Business logic stays in Python files, the presentation layer stays in templates, and neither layer needs to know about the other's implementation details. This makes future changes easier.

# Create your first flask app <a class="anchor" id="first"></a>

## 1. Create a `uv` project and install Flask <a class="anchor" id="install"></a>

This course uses `uv` for package management Wherever the Flask documentation or other tutorials say `flask run`, prefix it with `uv run` so that Flask runs inside the `uv` managed environment. We'll start by creating a dedicated directory for your Flask app and initialising a new `uv` project inside it. From inside the `week-03/` directory, run:

```bash
mkdir my_flask_app
cd my_flask_app
uv init
```

`uv init` creates a `pyproject.toml` and a `hello.py` starter file. You can delete `hello.py` — you will create `flaskapp.py` in the next step.

Now add Flask as a dependency:

```bash
uv add flask
```

This single command:

1. Resolves the Flask package and its dependencies
2. Adds `flask` to `pyproject.toml` under `[project] dependencies`
3. Creates (or updates) a `uv.lock` file pinning exact versions
4. Installs everything into a local `.venv` (no manual activation required)

## 2. Set-up your Flask app <a class="anchor" id="setup"></a>

Create a new Python file called `flaskapp.py` inside `my_flask_app/` containing:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
from flask import Flask   # <1>

app = Flask(__name__)     # <2>

@app.route("/")           # <3>
def home():               # <4>
    return "Home Page"    # <5>
```

1. Imports the `Flask` class from the `flask` package. Everything in a Flask app flows through an instance of this class.
2. Creates your application object. The `__name__` argument tells Flask where this module lives so it can find resources like templates and static files *relative to this file*. When you run `flaskapp.py` directly, `__name__` equals `"__main__"`; when it is imported, `__name__` is `"flaskapp"`. Either way, Flask resolves file paths correctly.
3. This is a **decorator** — a Python feature that wraps one function with another. Here, `app.route("/")` registers the decorated function in Flask's routing table: "when someone visits `/`, call `home()`." Decorators always appear directly above the function they modify, prefixed with `@`.
4. The **view function**. Flask calls this whenever the route is matched.
5. The return value becomes the HTTP response body. You can return plain text, HTML strings, or (later) rendered templates.

Note: Do not name it `flask.py`, as that would overlap with the Flask package itself and cause an import error.

## 3. Run your Flask app <a class="anchor" id="run"></a>

From inside `my_flask_app/`, start the development server:

```bash
uv run flask --app flaskapp run
```

If everything is working, your terminal will print something like:

```bash
 * Serving Flask app 'flaskapp'
 * Debug mode: off
WARNING: This is a development server. Do not use it in a production deployment.
 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000
Press CTRL+C to quit
```

Copy the URL `http://127.0.0.1:5000` into your browser. You should see "Home Page".

### What is `127.0.0.1:5000`?

Think of a network address like a building's postal address:

- `127.0.0.1` is the IP address — it means *this computer* (also called the **loopback address** or **localhost**). Traffic sent here never leaves your machine; it loops straight back to your own network stack. You can also type `localhost` instead.
- `5000` is the **port** — think of it as the apartment number within the building. Many programs can run on the same machine simultaneously, each listening on a different port. Flask defaults to 5000; if that port is taken, use `--port 5001`.

If you see `OSError: [Errno 48] Address already in use`, another process is already on port 5000. Run with a different port:

```bash
uv run flask --app flaskapp run --port 5001
```

### What does "Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000" mean under the hood?

Flask starts a WSGI development server (Werkzeug) that:

1. Binds to the port and listens for incoming (TCP) connections.
2. Parses each incoming HTTP request (method, URL, headers, body).
3. Sends incoming HTTP requests to the correct view functions using the routing table.
4. Serialises the return value from the view function into an HTTP response.
5. Returns the HTTP response to the requestor.

You can stop the server by pressing press Ctrl+C in the terminal.

## 4. Make some first changes to your app <a class="anchor" id="changes"></a>

### Adding HTML to the response

Replace `"Home Page"` in the `home` function with an HTML heading:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
return "<h1>Home Page</h1>"
```

HTML **tags** like `<h1>` tell the browser how to render text. Some common tags include:

- `<h1>` is the largest heading
- `<p>` is a paragraph
- `<b>` is bold.

After saving, you need to restart the server to see the change:

1. Stop it with Ctrl+C
2. Re-run `uv run flask --app flaskapp run`
3. Refresh your browser

This gets tedious quickly, which is why Flask has debug mode.

### Debug Mode (Auto-Reload)

Debug mode does two things:

1. **Hot-reload:** Flask watches your `.py` files for changes and restarts the server automatically when you save one of them. You only need to refresh the browser instead of manually restarting the server.
2. **Interactive debugger:** If your code raises an exception, the browser shows a detailed **traceback** with an interactive console (instead of a generic "500 Internal Server Error").

Never run debug mode in production. The interactive debugger allows arbitrary Python code execution in the browser, so only enable it during development on your local machine.

Enable debug mode by adding `--debug` to the run command:

```bash
uv run flask --app flaskapp run --debug
```

Your terminal will now show:

```bash
 * Debug mode: on
 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000
 * Restarting with stat
 * Debugger is active!
```

Alternatively, you can add these lines to the bottom of `flaskapp.py` and run the file directly with Python:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True)
```

Then:

```bash
uv run python flaskapp.py
```

The `if __name__ == '__main__':` ensures that `app.run()` is only called when you run the script directly, not when Flask imports it as a module.

## 5. Create new pages with routing <a class="anchor" id="newpages"></a>

**Routing** is the process of matching an incoming URL to the right Python function. Each `@app.route(...)` call registers one mapping in Flask's routing table.

Add a second page by pasting this into `flaskapp.py`:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/hello")
def hello():
    return "<h1>Hello, Berlin!</h1>"
```

The routing table now looks like this:

```
URL path        View function     Response
/               home()            "Home Page"
/hello          hello()           "<h1>Hello, Berlin!</h1>"
```

Visit `http://localhost:5000/hello` to see your new page.

### How routing works step by step

1. You type `http://localhost:5000/hello` in the browser.
2. The browser sends `GET /hello HTTP/1.1` to Flask.
3. Flask scans its routing table for a pattern that matches `/hello`.
4. It finds `@app.route("/hello")` and calls `hello()`.
5. `hello()` returns the HTML string.
6. Flask wraps it in an HTTP 200 response and sends it back.

### Multiple routes to the same page

If you want both `/` and `/home` to serve the home page, stack decorators:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/")
@app.route("/home")
def home():
    return "<h1>Home Page</h1>"
```

Flask will call `home()` for either URL.

Every view function name must be unique. If you define two functions both called `home`, Python will silently overwrite the first one and Flask will only register the second. Use distinct names like `home`, `about`, `hello`, etc.

## 6. Pass variables through the URL <a class="anchor" id="variables"></a>

Static routes like `/hello` always return the same page. **URL variables** let you pass data to the view function directly in the URL path, making pages **dynamic**.

Update your `hello` route in `flaskapp.py`:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/hello/<city>")
def hello(city):
    return f"<h1>Hello, {city}!</h1>"
```

Now try visiting:

- `http://localhost:5000/hello/Berlin` should give "Hello, Berlin!"
- `http://localhost:5000/hello/London` should give "Hello, London!"
- `http://localhost:5000/hello/NewYork` should give "Hello, NewYork!"

### How it works

Angle brackets in the route pattern (`<city>`) are **variable segments**. Flask extracts whatever string appears at that position in the URL and passes it as a keyword argument to the view function. The parameter name in the route (`city`) must match the parameter name in the function signature (`def hello(city)`).

### Type converters

By default, URL variables are captured as strings. Flask supports **type converters** that **validate** and convert the value automatically:

| Converter | Example route | Captured type |
|---|---|---|
| (none / default) | `<name>` | `str` |
| `int` | `<int:n>` | `int` (rejects non-integers with 404) |
| `float` | `<float:x>` | `float` |
| `path` | `<path:subpath>` | `str` including `/` characters |

You will use `<int:n>` in the Fibonacci route in the preview section. It ensures the value `n` is a valid integer before it reaches your Python function.

### URL encoding

Spaces and special characters are not allowed in URLs. The browser automatically encodes them:

- space becomes `%20`
- `/hello/New York` becomes `/hello/New%20York`

Flask decodes `%20` back to a space before passing the value to your function, so `city` will equal `"New York"` (with a space) in Python.

## 7. Add more content using templates <a class="anchor" id="templates"></a>

Embedding HTML directly in Python return statements works for trivial pages, but it scales poorly. Flask solves this with **templates**, which separate HTML files that can contain placeholders for **dynamic content**.

The template engine Flask uses is called **Jinja2** (the same engine used by many other tools). You write HTML as normal and include `{{ variable }}` placeholders and `{% control flow %}` blocks wherever you need Python data.

### Setting up the template directory

Flask automatically looks for templates in a folder called `templates/` that lives in the same directory as your `flaskapp.py` file.

In other words, your directory structure should be:

```bash
my_flask_app/
|-- pyproject.toml
|-- flaskapp.py
|-- templates/
    |-- home.html
```

If the `templates/` folder is in the wrong place (e.g. inside a sub-directory), Flask will raise a `TemplateNotFound` error.

### Step 1: Create `templates/home.html`

```{.html filename="templates/home.html"}
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Home</title>
    <meta name="description" content="My home page, on which I showcase my work!">
    <meta name="keywords" content="personal home page">
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Home Page</h1>
    This is where you will put the content of this page.
</body>
</html>
```

This is a minimal valid HTML document. Every HTML file has the same boilerplate:

- `<!doctype html>`: tells the browser this is HTML5
- `<head>`: metadata (title, CSS, etc.) that is not visible on the page
- `<body>`: everything visible in the browser window

### Step 2: Update `flaskapp.py` to render the template

Add `render_template` to your import and update the `home` function:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
from flask import Flask, render_template   # add render_template

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route("/")
@app.route("/home")
def home():
    return render_template('home.html')    # render the template file
```

`render_template('home.html')` tells Flask to:

1. Find `templates/home.html` relative to your app.
2. Process any Jinja2 placeholders.
3. Return the resulting HTML string as the response body.

## 8. Passing variables from Python to the template

You can pass any Python data into a template by providing keyword arguments to `render_template`. Jinja2 makes those values available inside the HTML.

### Step 1: Update the view function

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/home")
def home():
    return render_template('home.html', heading='My personal home page')
```

The string `'My personal home page'` is now bound to the name `heading` inside the template.

### Step 2: Display the variable in the template

In `home.html`, replace the static `<h1>` with a Jinja2 expression:

```{.html filename="templates/home.html"}
<h1>{{ heading }}</h1>
```

`{{ ... }}` is Jinja2's **output tag** — it evaluates the expression inside and inserts the result as text.

### Jinja2 Syntax Quick Reference

Jinja2 has three tag types:

| Syntax | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `{{ expr }}` | Output a value | `{{ heading }}` |
| `{% statement %}` | Control flow | `{% if %}`, `{% for %}`, `{% extends %}` |
| `{# comment #}` | Template comment (not sent to browser) | `{# TODO: add nav #}` |

### Conditional rendering

Make the heading optional — if `heading` is not passed, fall back to a default:

```{.html filename="templates/home.html"}
<body>
{% if heading %}
    <h1>{{ heading }}</h1>
{% else %}
    <h1>Home Page</h1>
{% endif %}
    This is where you will put the content of this page.
</body>
```

Jinja2 treats `None`, `""`, `0`, `[]`, and `{}` all as falsy like Python (recall that everything in Python is truthy).

### Looping over a list

You can pass Python objects, such as lists and dicts, and loop over them in the template. This is useful for displaying results:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/home")
def home():
    items = ["Sort results", "Search results", "Graph paths"]
    return render_template('home.html', heading='My App', items=items)
```

```{.html filename="templates/home.html"}
<ul>
{% for item in items %}
    <li>{{ item }}</li>
{% endfor %}
</ul>
```

This renders a `<ul>` with one `<li>` per item in the list, which is exactly how you will display algorithm output in later exercises.

## 9. Some very basic HTML and CSS  <a class="anchor" id="html"></a>

Now let's add some very basic styling to the home page by adapting the HTML code and adding some CSS

1. Wrap the text that you have placed within the `body` tag in a `div` tag and assign it a `class` called `'myDiv'`:

```{.html filename="templates/home.html"}
<body>
<div class="myDiv">
{% if heading %}
    <h1>{{ heading }}</h1>
{% else %}
    <h1>Home Page</h1>
{% endif %}
    This is where you will put the content of this page.
</div>
</body>
```

2. Add some CSS styling into a style tag within the head tag:

```{.html filename="templates/home.html"}
<head>
    <title>Home</title>
    <meta name="description" content="My home page, on which I showcase my work!">
    <meta name="keywords" content="personal home page">
    <style>
        .myDiv {
        border: solid darkblue 5px;
        padding-bottom: 20px;
        background-color: lightblue;
        text-align: center;
        font-family: Sans-serif;
        }
    </style>
</head>
```

Refresh your page and see how it has changed!

## Connecting Flask to Algorithms: A Preview

Here's a taste of how Flask will connect to actual Python code, such as an algorithm. Add this route to your app:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/fibonacci/<int:n>")
def fibonacci_api(n):
    """Return the nth Fibonacci number as JSON."""
    if n < 0:
        return {"error": "n must be non-negative"}, 400

    # Simple iterative Fibonacci (O(n) time, O(1) space)
    if n <= 1:
        return {"n": n, "fibonacci": n}

    prev, curr = 0, 1
    for _ in range(2, n + 1):
        prev, curr = curr, prev + curr

    return {"n": n, "fibonacci": curr}
```

Now visit `http://localhost:5000/fibonacci/10` in your browser!

This demonstrates:

- URL parameters with type conversion (`<int:n>`)
- Error handling (returning 400 for invalid input)
- JSON responses (Flask automatically converts dicts to JSON)

#### Question: What HTTP status code should we return for invalid input, and why?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Answer

We return 400 (Bad Request) because the client provided invalid input (negative number). This follows REST conventions:

- 200: Success
- 400: Client error (bad input)
- 404: Resource not found
- 500: Server error

Proper status codes help API consumers handle errors programmatically.
:::

# Exercises

### Exercise 1: Create Your First Flask App

Go through the steps above (sections 1-9) to create your first Flask app.

Verification checklist:

- [ ] Ran `uv add flask` from the `week-03/` directory
- [ ] Created `flaskapp.py` with a basic route
- [ ] Successfully ran the app with `uv run flask --app flaskapp run`
- [ ] Viewed your app at `http://localhost:5000`
- [ ] Enabled debug mode for auto-reloading
- [ ] Created a `/hello` route with a city variable
- [ ] Created a `templates/` directory with `home.html`
- [ ] Used `render_template()` to serve the template

#### Comprehension Check: Why do we use `render_template()` instead of returning HTML strings directly?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Answer

Using `render_template()` separates presentation (HTML) from logic (Python), making code:

1. More maintainable - HTML changes don't require Python changes
2. More reusable - Templates can be shared across routes
3. More powerful - Jinja2 provides loops, conditionals, inheritance
4. Cleaner - No messy string concatenation in Python
:::

### Exercise 2: Handle Missing URL Parameters

Try going to `http://localhost:5000/hello` in your browser after having completed step 6. How could you update the `hello` function so that it does not raise a "page not found" error?

#### Solution

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution

The easiest (but not very scalable) way to do this is within your `.py` file:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/hello")
@app.route("/hello/<city>")
def hello(city=None):
    if city is None:
        return "<h1>Hello, there!</h1>"
    else:
        return f"<h1>Hello, {city}!</h1>"
```

Alternatively (and this would be the more elegant and scalable way to do this) is to deal with the case of the city variable not being specified (aka being `None`) in a template. In this case, your `hello` function would look like this:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/hello")
@app.route("/hello/<city>")
def hello(city=None):
    return render_template('hello.html', city=city)
```

You then also need to create a `hello.html` template within the `templates` folder, into which you write:

```{.html filename="templates/hello.html"}
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Hello</title>
    <meta name="description" content="The page where you say hello.">
    <meta name="keywords" content="some keywords">
</head>
<body>
{% if city %}
    <h1>Hello, {{ city }}!</h1>
{% else %}
    <h1>Hello, there!</h1>
{% endif %}
</body>
</html>
```

:::

### Exercise 3: Add an About Page

Add an 'about' page to your app, which shows the text 'About Page' in an `h1` tag, by adding a suitable function to the `flaskapp.py` script (firstly, without using templates).

#### Solution

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/about")
def about():
    return "<h1>About Page</h1>"
```

:::

### Exercise 4: Create an About Template

Create a new template specifically for this new 'about' page by copying and pasting the HTML code from step 7 above and changing the parts that refer to it being the 'home' page, then update `flaskapp.py` accordingly.

#### Solution

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution

Create `templates/about.html`:

```{.html filename="templates/about.html"}
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>About</title>
    <meta name="description" content="My about page, where I introduce myself!">
    <meta name="keywords" content="about page">
</head>
<body>
    <h1>About Page</h1>
    This is where you will put the content of this page.
</body>
</html>
```

Update `flaskapp.py`:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/about")
def about():
    return render_template('about.html')
```

:::

### Exercise 5: Navigation Bar with Template Inheritance

This exercise introduces **template inheritance**, a powerful Flask/Jinja2 feature for avoiding code duplication.

#### Step 5.1: Understand the Problem

Currently, if you want a navigation bar on every page, you'd have to copy the HTML to each template. What if you have 10 pages? 100? Template inheritance solves this.

#### Step 5.2: Create the Base Layout

Create `templates/layout.html`:

```{.html filename="templates/layout.html"}
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>{% block title %}My App{% endblock %}</title>
</head>
<body>
    <nav>
        <a href="{{ url_for('home') }}">Home</a>
        <!-- TODO: Add more links -->
    </nav>

    <main>
        {% block content %}
        <!-- Child templates insert content here -->
        {% endblock %}
    </main>
</body>
</html>
```

Key concepts:

- `{% block name %}...{% endblock %}` defines replaceable sections
- `{{ url_for('function_name') }}` generates URLs dynamically

#### Step 5.3: Update home.html to Inherit

Modify `templates/home.html`:

```{.html filename="templates/home.html"}
{% extends "layout.html" %}

{% block title %}Home - My App{% endblock %}

{% block content %}
<h1>{{ heading }}</h1>
<p>Welcome to the home page!</p>
{% endblock %}
```

#### Step 5.4: Your Task

1. Add an "About" link to the navigation in `layout.html`
2. Update `about.html` to extend `layout.html`
3. Add basic CSS styling to the nav (horizontal layout, hover effects)

#### Hint: Making nav links horizontal with CSS

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Hint

```css
nav {
    background-color: #333;
    padding: 10px;
}
nav a {
    color: white;
    text-decoration: none;
    margin-right: 15px;
}
nav a:hover {
    text-decoration: underline;
}
```
:::

#### Full Solution

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Full Solution

```{.html filename="templates/layout.html"}
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>{% block title %}My App{% endblock %}</title>
    <style>
        nav { background-color: #333; padding: 10px; }
        nav a { color: white; text-decoration: none; margin-right: 15px; }
        nav a:hover { text-decoration: underline; }
        main { padding: 20px; }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <nav>
        <a href="{{ url_for('home') }}">Home</a>
        <a href="{{ url_for('about') }}">About</a>
    </nav>
    <main>
        {% block content %}{% endblock %}
    </main>
</body>
</html>
```

```{.html filename="templates/about.html"}
{% extends "layout.html" %}

{% block title %}About - My App{% endblock %}

{% block content %}
<h1>About Page</h1>
<p>This is the about page.</p>
{% endblock %}
```
:::

#### Comprehension Check: What's the advantage of template inheritance over copy-pasting?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Answer

Template inheritance follows the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle:

1. Single source of truth - Change the nav once, it updates everywhere
2. Consistency - All pages automatically share the same structure
3. Maintainability - Fixing a bug in the layout fixes it for all pages
4. Scalability - Adding 100 pages doesn't mean copying 100 nav bars
:::

### Exercise 6: Fibonacci Calculator Page

Build a Fibonacci Calculator page that connects the `fibonacci_api` route from the preview section to an HTML interface. This exercise combines everything from the lab: routes, URL parameters, templates, and template inheritance.

#### Step 6.1: Add the API Route

Make sure your `flaskapp.py` includes the Fibonacci API route from the preview section:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/fibonacci/<int:n>")
def fibonacci_api(n):
    """Return the nth Fibonacci number as JSON."""
    if n < 0:
        return {"error": "n must be non-negative"}, 400

    if n <= 1:
        return {"n": n, "fibonacci": n}

    prev, curr = 0, 1
    for _ in range(2, n + 1):
        prev, curr = curr, prev + curr

    return {"n": n, "fibonacci": curr}
```

Visit `http://localhost:5000/fibonacci/10` to confirm it returns JSON like `{"fibonacci": 55, "n": 10}`.

#### Step 6.2: Add a Landing Page Route

The API route returns JSON, but we need a separate route that renders an HTML form. Add this to `flaskapp.py`:

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py"}
@app.route("/fibonacci")
def fibonacci_page():
    return render_template("fibonacci.html")
```

#### Step 6.3: Create the Template

Create `templates/fibonacci.html`. The form submits to the API route using a GET request, but since the API expects the number in the URL (not as a query parameter), we'll use a small JavaScript redirect:

```{.html filename="templates/fibonacci.html"}
{% extends "layout.html" %}

{% block title %}Fibonacci Calculator{% endblock %}

{% block content %}
<h1>Fibonacci Calculator</h1>
<p>Enter a non-negative integer to calculate its Fibonacci number.</p>

<form onsubmit="goToResult(event)">
    <label for="n">n:</label>
    <input type="number" id="n" name="n" min="0" required>
    <button type="submit">Calculate</button>
</form>

<div id="result"></div>

<script>
function goToResult(event) {
    event.preventDefault();
    const n = document.getElementById("n").value;
    fetch("/fibonacci/" + n)
        .then(response => response.json())
        .then(data => {
            if (data.error) {
                document.getElementById("result").innerText = "Error: " + data.error;
            } else {
                document.getElementById("result").innerText =
                    "fibonacci(" + data.n + ") = " + data.fibonacci;
            }
        });
}
</script>
{% endblock %}
```

#### Step 6.4: Your Task

1. Add the `fibonacci_page` route to `flaskapp.py`
2. Create `templates/fibonacci.html` extending `layout.html`
3. Add a "Fibonacci" link to the nav in `layout.html`
4. Test it: visit `http://localhost:5000/fibonacci`, enter a number, and verify the result

#### Full Solution

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Full Solution

```{.python filename="flaskapp.py (additions)"}
@app.route("/fibonacci")
def fibonacci_page():
    return render_template("fibonacci.html")

@app.route("/fibonacci/<int:n>")
def fibonacci_api(n):
    if n < 0:
        return {"error": "n must be non-negative"}, 400
    if n <= 1:
        return {"n": n, "fibonacci": n}
    prev, curr = 0, 1
    for _ in range(2, n + 1):
        prev, curr = curr, prev + curr
    return {"n": n, "fibonacci": curr}
```

```{.html filename="templates/layout.html (updated nav)"}
<nav>
    <a href="{{ url_for('home') }}">Home</a>
    <a href="{{ url_for('about') }}">About</a>
    <a href="{{ url_for('fibonacci_page') }}">Fibonacci</a>
</nav>
```

```{.html filename="templates/fibonacci.html"}
{% extends "layout.html" %}

{% block title %}Fibonacci Calculator{% endblock %}

{% block content %}
<h1>Fibonacci Calculator</h1>
<p>Enter a non-negative integer to calculate its Fibonacci number.</p>

<form onsubmit="goToResult(event)">
    <label for="n">n:</label>
    <input type="number" id="n" name="n" min="0" required>
    <button type="submit">Calculate</button>
</form>

<div id="result"></div>

<script>
function goToResult(event) {
    event.preventDefault();
    const n = document.getElementById("n").value;
    fetch("/fibonacci/" + n)
        .then(response => response.json())
        .then(data => {
            if (data.error) {
                document.getElementById("result").innerText = "Error: " + data.error;
            } else {
                document.getElementById("result").innerText =
                    "fibonacci(" + data.n + ") = " + data.fibonacci;
            }
        });
}
</script>
{% endblock %}
```
:::

#### Comprehension Check: Why does the API route return JSON while the template route returns HTML?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Answer

Flask handles return values differently based on type:

- When a route returns a **dict**, Flask automatically converts it to a JSON response with `Content-Type: application/json`
- When a route calls `render_template(...)`, Flask returns an **HTML response** with `Content-Type: text/html`

Having two separate routes — one for JSON (`/fibonacci/<int:n>`) and one for HTML (`/fibonacci`) — is a common pattern. The HTML page can call the JSON API via JavaScript (as this exercise does with `fetch()`), keeping the logic in one place while supporting both browser users and programmatic API consumers.
:::
